bibliographic_data.title: Reference-free Hallucination Detection for Large Vision-Language Models
bibliographic_data.abstract: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have made significant progress in recent years. While LVLMs exhibit excellent ability in language understanding, question answering, and conversations of visual inputs, they are prone to producing hallucinations. Most existing methods of assessing hallucinations rely on external models. To assess the viability of alternative methods, it is critical to understand whether reference-free approaches can efficiently detect hallucinations. Therefore, we initiate an exploratory study to demonstrate the effectiveness of different reference-free solutions in detecting hallucinations in LVLMs. In particular, we conduct an extensive study on three kinds of techniques: uncertainty-based, consistency-based, and supervised uncertainty quantification methods on four representative LVLMs across two different tasks. The empirical results show that the reference-free approaches are capable of effectively detecting non-factual responses in LVLMs, with the supervised uncertainty quantification method outperforming the others, achieving the best performance across different settings.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper demonstrates that reference-free approaches can effectively detect hallucinations in large vision-language models (LVLMs), with supervised uncertainty quantification method outperforming others.
core_content.key_methodology: The study evaluates three kinds of techniques: uncertainty-based, consistency-based, and supervised uncertainty quantification methods on four representative LVLMs across two different tasks.
outcomes: Reference-free approaches can effectively detect non-factual responses in LVLMs.
Supervised uncertainty quantification method outperforms other methods across various settings.
core_content.full_text_markdown: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) like LLaVA and MiniGPT-4 have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in understanding and generating complex visual and textual content. However, an emerging concern with these models is their tendency towards hallucination. Most existing methods of assessing hallucinations rely on external models, which increases computational cost and restricts practical application. To address this issue, the paper initiates an exploratory study to demonstrate the effectiveness of different reference-free solutions in detecting hallucinations in LVLMs. The study evaluates three kinds of techniques: uncertainty-based, consistency-based, and supervised uncertainty quantification methods on four representative LVLMs across two different tasks. The empirical results show that the reference-free approaches are capable of effectively detecting non-factual responses in LVLMs, with the supervised uncertainty quantification method outperforming the others, achieving the best performance across different settings.
